phrasalVerbs.definition: To find or see someone or something when you did not expect to.
examples: While researching the archives, I chanced upon a letter that completely changed our understanding of the case.
She chanced upon her former professor at an international conference in Vienna.
The discovery was made when scientists chanced upon an unusual pattern in the data.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To laugh a lot or make someone laugh a lot.
examples: His deadpan delivery of the anecdote had the entire audience creasing up.
The children creased up when their grandfather started his impersonations.
Her witty observations about office life never fail to crease me up.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To trick someone into believing something that is not true.
examples: Sophisticated investors were taken in by the elaborate Ponzi scheme despite numerous warning signs.
The forgery was so convincing that even expert art historians were taken in by it.
Don't be taken in by his charming demeanor—he has ulterior motives.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To discuss something in a general way and without dealing with the most important issues.
examples: The committee spent hours talking round the subject without reaching any concrete conclusions.
Politicians are adept at talking round controversial topics to avoid committing to a position.
Stop talking round the issue and tell me directly what you think we should do.

fillInExercises.sentence: While browsing the antique shop, I ___ a rare first edition worth thousands.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Chance upon' means to find something unexpectedly by accident.

fillInExercises.sentence: His impression of the CEO had everyone in the meeting ___ up with laughter.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Crease up' means to laugh uncontrollably or make others laugh.

fillInExercises.sentence: Experienced investors should be wary of being ___ by promises of guaranteed returns.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take in' (in this context) means to be deceived or tricked.

fillInExercises.sentence: The negotiators kept ___ round the central dispute without addressing it directly.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Talk round' means to discuss superficially without addressing core issues.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ an old photograph of my grandparents while clearing out the attic.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Chance upon' means to discover something by accident.

fillInExercises.sentence: Even experienced collectors were ___ in by the sophisticated forgery.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take in' means to deceive someone successfully.

fillInExercises.sentence: Her sardonic wit had the entire dinner party ___ within minutes.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Crease up' means to cause someone to laugh intensely.

fillInExercises.sentence: Please stop ___ round the problem and give me a straight answer.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Talk round' means to avoid directly addressing an issue.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: You'll never believe what I [chanced upon] at the estate sale yesterday.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: What? Don't keep me in suspense—tell me!

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: An original manuscript! Though I hope I haven't been [taken in] by a clever fake.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: That would be quite embarrassing. Have you had it authenticated?

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: The appraiser was [talking round] the question of value, which makes me nervous.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: At least the story [creased up] everyone at the office when you told them!

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: My uncle was completely [taken in] by an investment scam last year.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: That's terrible. How did he [chance upon] these fraudsters in the first place?

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: He met them at a networking event. They were very persuasive.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: These con artists are skilled at [talking round] difficult questions.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: Now he can finally [crease up] about it, though at the time it was devastating.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Don't be too hard on him—anyone can be [taken in] by a sophisticated scheme.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: True. We've all [chanced upon] situations that tested our judgment.
